headline: CRUDE EXPLODES 7% — GRAINS SLIP ON PROFIT-TAKING
subheadline: Energy surge adds $15/acre to input costs while corn and beans ease after recent rally.
lead: Crude oil exploded 7% higher to $112.06 overnight, the biggest energy move in months, while grains retreated modestly with corn down 5¢ to $4.52 and soybeans off 9¼¢ to $11.63. The energy spike threatens to add $15-20 per acre to diesel and fertilizer costs just as corn planting begins across the upper Midwest. Meanwhile, grains are taking a breather after strong March gains, with December corn still holding above $4.81 despite today's pullback.
teaser: Energy costs explode while grains pause — timing couldn't be worse for planting season.

one_number.context: This 7% overnight surge is the biggest energy move in months, hitting right as corn planting season begins. Every $10 move in crude translates to roughly 25¢ per gallon at the diesel pump, adding $10-15 per acre to field operations. With optimal planting windows opening in two weeks, this couldn't come at a worse time for input costs.

sections.title: Grains Pause Rally
sections.body: Corn slipped 5¢ to $4.52 while soybeans dropped 9¼¢ to $11.63, taking profits after solid March gains. Chicago wheat fell 6½¢ to $5.98, pressured by improving Black Sea crop prospects. The retreat feels more like position squaring than fundamental weakness — December corn is still holding $4.81, well above seasonal lows. Managed money (hedge funds) likely took some chips off the table after riding the spring rally, but underlying demand from ethanol and exports remains firm.
sections.bottom_line: Healthy pullback after strong March — fundamentals still supportive.
sections.farmer_action: Hold current positions — this looks like profit-taking, not trend change. Watch Monday for buying interest on the dip.

sections.title: Livestock Mixed Signals
sections.body: Live cattle jumped 0.8% to $246.20, now sitting at 97% of their 52-week range as beef demand stays strong despite high prices. Feeders gained 0.7% to $370.67, but rising feed costs from higher energy are starting to squeeze margins. Lean hogs slipped 0.7% to $104.45 while Class III milk rallied 1.2% to $18.14. The energy spike creates a two-way headwind — higher feed costs but also stronger ethanol demand that supports corn basis.
sections.bottom_line: Energy surge creates margin pressure despite strong protein demand.
sections.farmer_action: Cattle feeders should price protection on current positions — feed cost inflation accelerating.

sections.title: Energy Shock
sections.body: WTI crude exploded 7% overnight to $112.06, the most significant energy move in months as geopolitical tensions escalated. This translates directly to $15-20 per acre in additional diesel and fertilizer costs just as corn planting begins. Natural gas dropped 2% to $2.81, providing some relief for nitrogen costs, but diesel is the bigger concern for spring field work. Soybean oil gained 1.6% to $69.00, benefiting from both the energy surge and biodiesel demand.
sections.bottom_line: Energy explosion hits at worst possible time — planting season.
sections.farmer_action: Lock diesel prices now if you haven't already — this energy spike has legs and spring demand is about to peak.

sections.title: Dollar Holds Steady
sections.body: The US Dollar Index barely budged at $100.00, remaining neutral for agricultural exports despite the energy-driven inflation spike. A stable dollar keeps US grain competitive globally while energy costs surge worldwide. The S&P 500 gained just 0.1% to $6,582, showing remarkable calm despite crude's explosion. Bitcoin dropped 1.6% to $66,982 as traditional energy assets claimed the spotlight. Gold slipped fractionally to $4,703, surprisingly weak given the inflationary energy shock.
sections.bottom_line: Stable dollar supports grain exports despite energy-driven cost inflation.
sections.farmer_action: No currency hedging needed — dollar stability supports competitive export pricing.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Energy Spikes Hit Farmers Hardest in Spring
the_more_you_know.body: Spring fieldwork is the most diesel-intensive period of the farming calendar, with corn planting requiring 3-5 gallons per acre between tillage, planting, and early cultivation. A $20 jump in crude oil translates to roughly 50¢ per gallon at the diesel pump, adding $2.50 per acre just in fuel costs. But the bigger hit comes from fertilizer — natural gas feeds ammonia production while diesel powers distribution, creating a double whammy that can add $15-30 per acre to nitrogen costs when energy spikes during application season.

watch_list.desc: Monitor geopolitical developments driving crude oil — sustainability of 7% spike key for input cost planning.
USDA monthly supply/demand estimates — corn stocks and new-crop yield assumptions in focus.
Upper Midwest weather models for mid-April — planting window timing becomes critical with higher input costs.
Optimal corn planting window opens — every day of delay now costs more with elevated energy prices.

daily_quote.text: The difference between a 180-bushel corn crop and a 230-bushel crop is usually management, not genetics.

source_summary: CME Group · NYMEX · Yahoo Finance · USDA · Open-Meteo
